original_abstract: This paper explores the multifaceted challenges of academic publishing in the digital age, with a particular focus on the accessibility and distribution of scholarly content. By examining the shift from traditional print media to restricted online repositories, we analyze the systemic barriers that currently limit the free exchange of scientific knowledge. Our findings suggest that current licensing frameworks often hinder the dissemination of critical research, creating a gap between data availability and researcher needs. Consequently, this study proposes a more integrated approach to open-access protocols, emphasizing the necessity of streamlining institutional access to ensure that the scientific community remains informed and collaborative.
humanized_abstract: Academic publishing today presents a multitude of challenges in a digital era; particularly with respect to scholarly communication and access to scholarly literature. Through examination of the barriers that have been imposed with the move from print to closed access on-line platforms, this paper investigates obstacles to the unencumbered dissemination of scientific information. It finds that present-day licensing restrictions severely hinder the dissemination of the most critical research, creating a disconnect between information availability and researcher requirements. This has motivated the work here proposing a more holistic approach to open access publishing practices with a specific recommendation to take measures to better align institutional access points so as to facilitate ongoing communicative interaction within the scientific community.
